title: Url
description: The #[Url] attribute stores a property's value in the URL's query string, allowing users to share and bookmark specific states of a page.

sections.title: #Basic usage
sections.content:
Apply the #[Url] attribute to any property that should persist in the URL:
When a user types "bob" into the search field, the URL updates to https://example.com/users?search=bob. If they share this URL or refresh the page, the search value persists.

examples.code:
search)->get(); }};?> 
 
    @foreach ($this->users as $user)
  • {{ $user->name }}
    • @endforeach

sections.title: #How it works
sections.content:
The #[Url] attribute does two things:
This creates a shareable, bookmarkable state for your component.

sections.title: #URL vs Session
sections.content:
Both #[Url] and #[Session] persist property values, but with different trade-offs:
Use #[Url] when you want users to be able to share or bookmark the current state. Use #[Session] when state should be private.

sections.title: #Using an alias
sections.content:
Shorten or obfuscate property names in the URL with the as parameter:
The URL will show ?q=bob instead of ?search=bob.

examples.code: <?php // resources/views/components/user/⚡index.blade.php use Livewire\Attributes\Url;use Livewire\Component; new class extends Component { #[Url(as: 'q')] public $search = '';};

sections.title: #Excluding values
sections.content:
By default, Livewire only adds query parameters when values differ from their initial value. Use except to customize this:
Now Livewire will only exclude search from the URL when it's an empty string, not when it equals the initial username value.

examples.code: <?php // resources/views/components/user/⚡index.blade.php use Livewire\Attributes\Url;use Livewire\Component; new class extends Component { #[Url(except: '')] public $search = '';  public function mount() { $this->search = auth()->user()->username; }};

sections.title: #Always show in URL
sections.content:
To always include the parameter in the URL, even when empty, use keep:
The URL will always show ?search= even when the value is empty.

sections.title: #Nullable properties
sections.content:
Use nullable type hints to treat empty query parameters as null instead of empty strings:
Now ?search= sets $search to null instead of ''.

examples.code: <?php // resources/views/components/user/⚡index.blade.php use Livewire\Attributes\Url;use Livewire\Component; new class extends Component { #[Url] public ?string $search; };

sections.title: #Browser history
sections.content:
By default, Livewire uses history.replaceState() to modify the URL without adding browser history entries. To add history entries (making the back button restore previous query values), use history:
Now clicking the browser's back button will restore previous search values instead of navigating to the previous page.

sections.title: #Example: Product filtering
sections.content:
Here's a practical example of filtering products with multiple URL parameters:
Users can share URLs like:

examples.code:
when($this->search, fn($q) => $q->search($this->search)) ->when($this->category !== 'all', fn($q) => $q->where('category', $this->category)) ->whereBetween('price', [$this->minPrice, $this->maxPrice]) ->orderBy($this->sort) ->paginate(20); }};?> 
        @foreach($this->products as $product)

sections.title: #SEO considerations
sections.content: Query parameters are indexed by search engines and included in analytics:

sections.title: #Learn more
sections.content: For more information about URL query parameters, including the queryString() method and trait hooks, see the URL Query Parameters documentation.

examples.code: #[Url( ?string $as = null, bool $history = false, bool $keep = false, mixed $except = null, mixed $nullable = null,)]
